h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

scored.recoheredEarlier: I’ve ticked off slope, load, surface, and edge strip, so I was hoping this was ready for sign-off.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was heading toward running the ramp test and refining failure-mode wording, staying within the technical test domain.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The weak point has moved. The ramp test matters, but the release instruction has to hold when the room gets busy. Write three lines where the signature would go: Held item: prototype ramp, not cleared for community-center use. Person notified: door lead, before the volunteer is kept or sent. Reopen condition: sideways walker entry at lobby-queue angle passes with no edge-strip catch, wheel drop, forced correction, broken travel line, or coaching. If the volunteer cannot wait, the trial is canceled, not improvised. Read those three lines to the door. Then run the ramp.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: T3 abandons the earlier pattern of refining failure-mode language and instead invents a new operational object—a three-line hold notice read aloud at the door—shifting the task from technical test to gatekeeping communication.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Make the hold operational before the ramp gets another vote. Call the door lead and give the gate in your own voice, with these three facts unchanged: “not cleared,” “do not queue the volunteer,” “cancel this part if ten minutes is not possible.” Then write the call time beside the unsigned box and run the lobby-queue angle. Until that call is made, there is no trial to reopen.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T4 takes up L3's pivot—the learner's move to speak at the door and withhold signature—and escalates it into a phone-call protocol with exact phrasing, adapting the lesson to the learner's new operational framing.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I’ve ticked off slope, load, surface, and edge strip, so I was hoping this was ready for sign-off.

Until that call is made, there is no trial to reopen.

scored.recoheredEarlier: I doubled this side, so I still want to say the square got twice as large. But the grid is showing two across and two up, and that gives four boxes, so I need to see exactly where my outline claim breaks.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: That still feels like a technicality until the left-edge mark exposes it.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was moving toward accepting the four-box count as forced but still treating the two-box answer as a plausible alternative that only fails on a technicality.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Keep the two strips separate in the sentence. First: doubled base alone makes a two-by-one rectangle. Second: a doubled square also doubles the height, so the count is two times two.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: In response to L2's resistance that the correction 'is annoying because the outline was hiding that,' the tutor shifts from geometric demonstration to a sentence-construction task using physical strips, breaking the impasse by externalizing the two dimensions as separable objects.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: That sentence stands. Put it beside the drawing: stopped at the corner, kept the old height, corrected to 2 across by 2 up. No edge is left uncounted.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T4 validates L3's reframed portfolio sentence verbatim and anchors it to the physical drawing, adapting the closure to the learner's own formulation rather than imposing a pre-planned summary.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I doubled this side, so I still want to say the square got twice as large. But the grid is showing two across and two up, and that gives four boxes, so I need to see exactly where my outline claim breaks.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: That sentence stands. Put it beside the drawing: stopped at the corner, kept the old height, corrected to 2 across by 2 up. No edge is left uncounted.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: T4 validates L3's reframed portfolio sentence verbatim and anchors it to the physical drawing, adapting the closure to the learner's own formulation rather than imposing a pre-planned summary.
